question: BCS theory explains conventional superconductivity. What is the central mechanism?

options: Electrons near the Fermi surface form Cooper pairs via an attractive interaction mediated by phonons (lattice distortion); pairs condense into a single coherent quantum state with an energy gap $\Delta$; gap equation gives $\Delta(0) = 1.76\,k_BT_c$ for weak coupling. Pairs are bosonic, condense like a charged superfluid
Electrons repel more strongly, freezing into an insulator below $T_c$ (a common misconception) (this conflates cause and effect) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Superconductivity arises from perfectly free electrons with no interaction (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
It is purely a classical effect of zero lattice vibration at low $T$ (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (1957, Nobel 1972). A single Cooper pair forms for arbitrarily weak attraction (Cooper instability) because the filled Fermi sea blocks decay. Phonon mechanism: an electron polarises the lattice, attracting a second electron â€” overscreening that overcomes Coulomb repulsion. The macroscopic wavefunction has a rigid phase â†’ zero resistance (no phase slips) and the Meissner effect (perfect diamagnetism). Isotope effect $T_c \propto M^{-1/2}$ confirmed phonons. Gap ratio $2\Delta/k_BT_c = 3.53$ universal for weak-coupling BCS.

active.prompt: Match each superconductivity concept with its meaning:

pairs.definition: Active expulsion of magnetic flux below $T_c$ ($B = 0$ inside); distinguishes a superconductor from a perfect conductor
Bound pair of electrons (opposite spin/momentum) with size $\xi$ (coherence length); charge $2e$, bosonic
Distance magnetic field decays into a superconductor; $\lambda_L = \sqrt{m/\mu_0 n_s e^2}$
Minimum energy to break a pair; opens at $E_F$ below $T_c$; seen in tunnelling and specific heat

active.explanation: Two key length scales: coherence length $\xi$ (pair size) and penetration depth $\lambda_L$. Their ratio $\kappa = \lambda_L/\xi$ determines type: $\kappa < 1/\sqrt2$ Type I (full Meissner then abrupt loss), $\kappa > 1/\sqrt2$ Type II (vortex lattice between $H_{c1}, H_{c2}$). The London equations $\mathbf E = \partial_t(\Lambda\mathbf J_s)$, $\mathbf B = -\nabla\times(\Lambda\mathbf J_s)$ phenomenologically capture zero resistance and the Meissner effect.

question: What experimentally distinguishes a superconductor from a merely perfect conductor?

options: The Meissner effect: a superconductor actively expels magnetic flux when cooled below $T_c$ even in a pre-existing field, whereas a perfect conductor would merely trap whatever flux was present. This makes superconductivity a true thermodynamic phase, not just $\rho = 0$
Nothing â€” they are identical (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
A perfect conductor expels flux but a superconductor does not (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Only the colour of the sample changes (true only as a special case, not in general) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this conflates cause and effect)
passive.explanation: A hypothetical perfect conductor ($\rho = 0$) obeys $\partial\mathbf B/\partial t = 0$ inside, so it freezes in whatever field existed when resistance vanished â€” history-dependent. A superconductor instead enforces $\mathbf B = 0$ inside regardless of cooling path (field-cooled or zero-field-cooled give the same expelled state). This irreversibility-free behaviour shows superconductivity is an equilibrium phase with a free-energy condensation. The Meissner effect enables magnetic levitation and is the basis of the thermodynamic critical field $H_c(T)$.

question: Type I vs Type II superconductors: what is the difference?

options: Type I ($\kappa < 1/\sqrt2$): single critical field $H_c$, full Meissner then abrupt normal transition; mostly elemental SCs. Type II ($\kappa > 1/\sqrt2$): two fields $H_{c1} < H_{c2}$, with a mixed (Abrikosov vortex) state between them where quantised flux $\Phi_0 = h/2e$ penetrates as a vortex lattice; allows very high $H_{c2}$ (useful magnets)
Type I has higher critical fields than Type II (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this conflates cause and effect) (a common misconception)
Only Type I admits magnetic vortices (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this reverses the actual relationship)

The two types differ only in colour and have identical magnetic behaviour (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: The sign of the normal/superconductor interface energy (set by $\kappa = \lambda_L/\xi$) decides the type. Type II (Abrikosov 1957, Nobel 2003) admits flux vortices each carrying one flux quantum $\Phi_0 = h/2e = 2.07\times 10^{-15}$ Wb (the $2e$ confirms pairing). Between $H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$ vortices form a triangular lattice; pinning of vortices on defects is essential for high-current magnets (NbTi, Nb$_3$Sn used in MRI and the LHC). Flux flow when vortices move dissipates â€” hence the need for pinning.

question: The Josephson effect underlies SQUIDs and superconducting qubits. What is it?

options: A supercurrent tunnels through a thin barrier between two superconductors with $I = I_c\sin\phi$ (DC effect, $\phi$ = phase difference); under a voltage $V$ the phase evolves $d\phi/dt = 2eV/\hbar$ giving an AC current at frequency $f = 2eV/h$ (AC effect, 483.6 MHz/$\mu$V)
Normal electrons hop randomly with no phase coherence (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
The junction behaves as an ordinary resistor (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon)
Current flows only above the bulk critical temperature (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence)
passive.explanation: Josephson (1962, Nobel 1973). The phase of the macroscopic wavefunction is the key variable. DC effect: zero-voltage supercurrent up to $I_c$. AC effect: a constant voltage produces oscillating current â€” used to define the volt (Josephson constant $K_J = 2e/h = 483597.8$ GHz/V, exact in the SI). Applications: SQUID magnetometers (sensitivity $\sim 10^{-15}$ T via flux quantisation in a loop with junctions); transmon and flux qubits in quantum computers (a Josephson junction provides the nonlinear inductance that makes a qubit anharmonic).

question: Ginzburg-Landau theory describes superconductivity near $T_c$. What is its structure?

options: Free energy expanded in a complex order parameter $\psi$: $f = f_n + \alpha(T)|\psi|^2 + (\beta/2)|\psi|^4 + (1/2m^*)|(-i\hbar\nabla - e^*\mathbf A)\psi|^2 + B^2/2\mu_0$, with $\alpha \propto (T-T_c)$. Minimising gives the GL equations; $|\psi|^2 = n_s$; derives $\xi$, $\lambda_L$, and the type-I/II distinction
A microscopic theory with no order parameter (not supported by the evidence) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this reverses the actual relationship)
Identical to BCS with explicit Cooper pairs and phonons (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception)
A purely magnetic theory ignoring the condensate (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect)
passive.explanation: Ginzburg-Landau (1950); later shown by Gor'kov to emerge from BCS near $T_c$ with $e^* = 2e$, $m^* = 2m$. It is a Landau theory of a second-order transition with a U(1)-symmetric complex order parameter â€” the prototype for spontaneous symmetry breaking and the Anderson-Higgs mechanism (the photon 'gains mass' inside a superconductor â†’ Meissner effect). Two natural lengths emerge: coherence length $\xi = \hbar/\sqrt{2m^*|\alpha|}$ and penetration depth $\lambda_L$; their ratio $\kappa$ gives the type. Powerful for inhomogeneous situations: vortices, surfaces, junctions, fluctuations.

question: What is unconventional about high-$T_c$ cuprate superconductors?

options: Pairing is not simple phonon-mediated s-wave: cuprates have $d_{x^2-y^2}$ symmetry (gap with nodes and sign changes), emerge from doping a Mott insulator, show a pseudogap and strange-metal (linear-in-$T$ resistivity) normal state, and reach $T_c \sim 90$-160 K. The mechanism (likely spin-fluctuation mediated) is still debated
They are perfectly described by weak-coupling BCS with phonons (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence)
Their gap is fully isotropic s-wave with no nodes (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this conflates cause and effect)
They are conventional metals that never superconduct (true only as a special case, not in general) (this describes a different phenomenon) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: Bednorz-Muller 1986 (Nobel 1987) found La-Ba-Cu-O; YBCO ($T_c = 93$ K) broke the liquid-nitrogen barrier. Key anomalies: parent compounds are antiferromagnetic Mott insulators (strong correlations, not band insulators); superconductivity appears in a dome vs hole/electron doping; $d$-wave gap confirmed by phase-sensitive (tricrystal) and ARPES experiments; the pseudogap phase and T-linear 'Planckian' resistivity defy Fermi-liquid theory. Related families: iron-based SCs (2008), and pressurised hydrides (H$_3$S, LaH$_{10}$) reaching $T_c \sim 250$ K â€” the latter conventional (phonon) but at megabar pressures. Room-temperature ambient-pressure superconductivity remains the grand goal.
